name: anu (sky)
shortDescription: Anu is the Babylonian god of the sky, representing the celestial realm and supreme authority. He is a key figure in the Babylonian pantheon and creation myths.
description: Anu, also known as An, is the ancient Mesopotamian god of the sky, heavens, and supreme authority. He is considered one of the oldest and most important deities in the Babylonian pantheon, often depicted as a distant, uninvolved ruler. Anu's origins trace back to Sumerian mythology, where he was known as An. He is the father of the gods, although his direct involvement in the world is limited compared to other deities. In the Enuma Elish, the Babylonian creation epic, Anu plays a significant role as the source of power and kingship. He is associated with the celestial sphere and represents the ultimate source of cosmic order. While he delegates much of his power to other gods like Enlil and Ea, Anu remains the ultimate authority, residing in the highest heavens. His role is more passive than active, representing the distant, unreachable aspect of the divine. He is often invoked in royal inscriptions as the grantor of kingship and legitimacy. His association with Tiamat is complex, as he is a descendant of the primordial chaos and is involved in the battles that establish cosmic order. He is also connected to Apsu, the primordial freshwater ocean, through the early stages of creation. The lack of direct interaction with the mortal realm contributed to his gradual decline in prominence over time as other gods, like Marduk, rose in power.

sources.title: The Religion of Babylonia and Assyria
sources.author: Theophilus G. Pinches

sources.title: Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament
sources.author: James B. Pritchard
